title: Re-interpret Story of Snow White: The Unsleeping Echo
author: Ng Chau Yi, Sharon

chapters.title: Chapter One: The Inherited Gaze
chapters.text:
The air in the chamber hung thick and sweet, heavy with the ghosts of burnt wax and an ancient, cloying vanity. Snow White found herself standing at the precipice of its echoing heart, a room sculpted entirely from obsidian mirrors and flickering candle flame. Each surface, from floor to ceiling, presented a fragmented, shifting version of herself, fractured by angles, elongated by shadows. The very act of moving felt like an exercise in parallax; the walls shimmered and tilted, her own reflection refusing to solidify, a thousand Snow Whites chasing an elusive center.

At the room's nexus, where the light seemed to converge into a swirling vortex, stood it. Not merely a looking-glass, but The Mirror. Her stepmother's legacy, still humming with a faint, malevolent energy, its frame a silver serpent devouring its own tail. It no longer held the cold, objective truth of glass, but pulsed with a warm, deceptive glow, inviting communion. This mirror, Snow White understood, was not for seeing but for being seen, for absorbing the gazes of generations, particularly the last, whose warped perception had stained its very essence. It was a repository of inherited trauma, a polished canvas for distorted self-image, whispering of an elusive perfection that always lay just beyond reach.

Snow White, herself a vision of fragile youth, felt the weight of its gaze. Her skin, kissed by moonlight, seemed too translucent; her hair, raven's wing, too stark against the pale, unending reflections. She reached out, her fingers wavering, ready to touch the cold, silent surface, to peer into the abyss of her own becoming. But what would it show her? Not what was, but what had been seen, what was expected. The mirror was no longer a tool of revelation; it was a conduit of a past, poisoned judgment, a labyrinth of selfhood where the only true exit was to step away, yet the room itself seemed to demand a confrontation. The flickering candle flames, multiplied into an infernal galaxy, danced like critical eyes, demanding a choice of recognition.
chapters.image_prompt:
Subject: Snow White, young woman, pale skin, jet-black hair, wearing a dark blue gothic fairytale dress.
Action: Standing at the entrance of a vast, candle-lit, obsidian mirror room, a look of apprehension and wonder on her face. Her form is slightly blurred and multiplied by the parallax effect of the mirrors.
Setting: A grand, circular chamber made entirely of dark, reflective obsidian mirrors. Hundreds of flickering candle flames are reflected infinitely, creating a disorienting, swirling galaxy of light. Deep shadows obscure distant corners.
Style: Gothic Illustration, Dark Fairytale Art, Surrealism, Chiaroscuro lighting, volumetric lighting, highly detailed, cinematic, painterly.
Details: The mirrors subtly distort her reflection; a faint, unsettling shimmer emanates from the room's center.

chapters.title: Chapter Two: The Forking Path
chapters.text:
From the heart of the inherited mirror, two offerings materialized, shimmering with an impossible, internal light. One, a polished sphere of crimson, glowed with an almost mythic allure. It was an apple, certainly, but unlike any grown in common earth. Its skin was unblemished, a perfect, dangerous ruby, hinting at secrets and consequences. Its very presence hummed with the ancient whisper of temptation, of forbidden knowledge disguised as succulent promise. It spoke of a clarity, a solution, a path to the absolute, even if that absolute was a precipice.

Beside it, in stark, almost impudent contrast, sat a towering confection of pure white. A mountain of ice cream, piled in an impossibly grand sundae glass, adorned with sprinkles that glittered like scattered jewels. It pulsed with a cool, comforting radiance, a siren song of modern solace and boundless excess. It promised oblivion, a velvety balm for anxieties, an endless, unearned gratification. Where the apple was a challenge, a dangerous truth, the ice cream was an escape, a soft surrender.

Snow White felt an invisible hand, an urge not her own, guiding her gaze, her mental faculties, towards these two impossibilities. The mirror-room's reflections blurred further, her own face splintering into expressions of acute indecision, of primal craving. The choice was not merely between fruit and cream, but between two forms of psychological collapse. Would she choose the sharp, bitter clarity of self-annihilation veiled as enlightenment? Or the sweet, suffocating embrace of a boundless, all-consuming void? Her heart, a small, trapped bird, beat a furious rhythm against her ribs. The weight of centuries of choosing, of women bound by such perilous dilemmas, pressed down upon her. The path she now took would irrevocably redraw the landscape of her internal world.
chapters.image_prompt:
Subject: Snow White, young woman, pale skin, jet-black hair, wearing a dark blue gothic fairytale dress.
Action: Reaching out with a trembling hand towards a large, ornate mirror at the room's nexus, her expression a mix of trepidation, curiosity, and slight fear. The mirror seems to hum with malevolent energy.
Setting: The heart of the obsidian mirror chamber. The large, central mirror's frame is a silver serpent devouring its own tail. The surrounding mirror surfaces reflect fragmented versions of her conflicted face.
Style: Gothic Illustration, Dark Fairytale Art, Surrealism, Chiaroscuro lighting, soft glowing light from the mirror, highly detailed, cinematic, painterly.
Details: The mirror glows with an inner, deceptive warmth; her reflection in it is subtly warped, hinting at a past, poisoned judgment; the air around her appears thick with unseen energy.

chapters.title: Chapter Three: The Crimson Bite
chapters.text:
Snow White chose the apple. Its skin, unnaturally perfect, felt cool beneath her fingertips, a deceptive promise of solace. As she lifted it, the mirrored walls seemed to lean in, reflecting her action a thousandfold, each image a silent, accusing witness. The air thickened, no longer just sweet with wax, but with the faint, metallic tang of anticipation.

The first bite was a revelation, sharp and clean. Not the anticipated bitterness of poison, but a burst of flavor both unfamiliar and deeply resonant, like a memory she couldn't quite place. A curious warmth spread through her, not unpleasant, but other. With each subsequent, deliberate mastication, the subtle disorientation began. The flame-multiplied candles flickered not in unison, but in erratic, independent rhythms, their light elongating shadows into grotesque caricatures of her own limbs. The edges of the mirrors, once sharp, seemed to ripple like water, blurring the lines between her reflection and the room itself.

Then came the first whisper of a hallucination, a delicate, almost playful intrusion. A tiny, obsidian bird, no bigger than her thumb, detached itself from one of the countless reflections and perched, for a fleeting moment, on the rim of the untouched ice cream sundae. Its eye, a bead of polished jet, winked at her with unsettling intelligence before dissolving back into the shimmering glass. Snow White blinked, shook her head, the apple still clutched in her hand, its core now exposed, revealing a surprising, almost luminous white flesh. Was it hunger, or something more insidious, that made her perception so malleable? She took another bite, a desperate attempt to ground herself in the tangible, but the world was already tilting. A faint, almost imperceptible shift in her own reflection in the nearest mirror caught her eye. Was her waistline truly so... wide? Her jawline so... soft? The apple, once a symbol of forbidden knowledge, now began to taste like a cruel, deceptive agent of internal sabotage, each mouthful feeding a nascent doubt that gnawed not at her stomach, but at the very fabric of her self-image.
chapters.image_prompt:
Subject: Snow White's hands, slender and delicate.
Action: Her hands hover between two glowing objects.
Setting: Emerging from the smoky, reflective surface of The Mirror, a perfect, gleaming crimson apple with a dark, mythical allure on the left, and a towering, impossibly white ice cream sundae, piled high with glittering sprinkles, on the right. Both radiate an internal, otherworldly light. The background is a blurred, shimmering reflection of the mirror room.
Style: Gothic Illustration, Dark Fairytale Art, Surrealism, Chiaroscuro lighting, extreme close-up, highly detailed, cinematic, painterly.
Details: The apple's skin is unnaturally smooth, almost glowing; the ice cream sparkles with a deceptive comfort; subtle distortions are visible in the reflections on the offerings themselves.

chapters.title: Chapter Four: The Tilted Garden
chapters.text:
The mirror room, no longer a sanctuary of reflections, buckled and spun. The obsidian surfaces dissolved not into darkness, but into a kaleidoscope of impossible flora: overgrown playing cards forming hedges, their painted faces leering; roses that bloomed with clockwork precision, their petals spinning like gears; and every surface, every shadow, seemed to hum with an unheard, nonsensical rhythm. Snow White found herself standing in a garden where logic was a forgotten language, and perspective a cruel jest.

The hallucinations intensified, no longer fleeting glances but fully immersive experiences. A Cheshire Cat, all grin and disembodied eyes, shimmered into existence on a bough of thorny cards, its laughter a sound like rustling parchment. "Which way, my dear?" it purred, its voice echoing from everywhere and nowhere, "To find what you seek, or to lose what you are?" Snow White stumbled, her sense of direction shattered, the ground beneath her feet shifting like an ocean swell. Birds, their feathers the color of spilled ink and forgotten dreams, swooped down, carrying messages scrawled on tiny, tattered scraps of silk. But the words were gibberish, an unholy mashup of commands and riddles that only deepened her confusion.

Her body, in this distorted landscape, felt increasingly alien. She caught glimpses of herself in the polished leaves of impossible plants, or in the reflective puddles of liquid time that gathered at her feet. Each reflection presented a different distortion: sometimes too thin, gaunt and stretched like a forgotten canvas; other times bloated, her limbs disproportionately large, her face a grotesque caricature of its former self. The apple, long since consumed, had become a ghost in her mind, an internalized acid that dissolved her perception of physical reality. She compulsively traced the contours of her own face, pinching at imaginary flaws, convinced that the world saw the grotesque image the mirrors now threw back at her. The breakdown of rational thought was not a sudden explosion, but a slow, insidious erosion, like sand slipping through an hourglass, each grain carrying a piece of her former self away.
chapters.image_prompt:
Subject: Snow White, young woman, pale skin, jet-black hair, wearing a dark blue gothic fairytale dress.
Action: Her face, fragmented and multiplied across dozens of mirror shards, each reflection showing a different nuance of intense indecision, internal conflict, and primal craving. Her eyes are wide, haunted by the gravity of the choice.
Setting: A close-up collage of fractured mirror reflections of Snow White's face, the background an abstract blur of flickering candlelight and deep shadows.
Style: Gothic Illustration, Dark Fairytale Art, Surrealism, Chiaroscuro lighting, fragmented portraiture, highly detailed, cinematic, painterly.
Details: One reflection shows desire for the apple, another for the ice cream; deep shadows emphasize the psychological turmoil; the edges of the mirror shards are sharp and angular.

chapters.title: Chapter Five: The Queen of Hearts' Reflection
chapters.text:
The shifting garden culminated in a colossal, crimson archway, framed by towering, animated poker cards. The Queen of Hearts, a monstrous figure fashioned from a thousand distorted reflections, sat upon a throne of entangled roses and jagged clock gears. Her face was a terrifying mosaic: the cruel, unyielding gaze of Snow White's stepmother, overlaid with the sharp, assessing eyes of every critical voice Snow White had ever internalized, distorted through the lens of her own self-loathing.

"Off with her head! Off with her senses!" the Queen shrieked, her voice a chorus of echoing self-doubt. The surrounding cards, forming a court of sneering courtiers, began to march, their movements erratic and menacing. The ace of spades, its grim motif magnified to an absurd degree, hovered ominously, threatening to descend. Snow White felt a tightening in her chest, a profound terror that was both external and deeply internal. This wasn't merely a hallucination; it was the raw, unvarnished fear of judgment, the crushing weight of perceived imperfection, given monstrous form.

She tried to rationalize, to find a pattern, a rule, in this chaotic domain. But the rules changed with every beat of her frantic heart. A path that was there one moment vanished the next, replaced by a wall of snarling card soldiers. The Cheshire Cat, now perched on the Queen's crown, chuckled, "The only rule here, my dear, is that there are no rules. Only perception." Snow White felt her mind fragmenting further, her thoughts scattering like a deck of cards thrown into a gale. Each absurd command from the Queen, each mocking whisper from the Cat, each shifting landscape, was a hammer blow against the fragile edifice of her sanity. She was not just trapped in a dream, but consumed by a living nightmare where her own insecurities were the architects of her torment, and the apple's poison was the very foundation of this reality.
chapters.image_prompt:
Subject: Snow White, young woman, pale skin, jet-black hair, wearing a dark blue gothic fairytale dress. Her expression is a mix of relief and nascent unease.
Action: Taking a deliberate first bite from the crimson apple. The apple's skin is perfectly peeled back, revealing luminous white flesh.
Setting: The mirror room, but the reflections are subtly warping. Shadows are elongating into grotesque, phantom limbs that mimic her own. The candlelight flickers erratically.
Style: Gothic Illustration, Dark Fairytale Art, Surrealism, Chiaroscuro lighting, subtle distortions in reflections, highly detailed, cinematic, painterly.
Details: The apple glows faintly where bitten; a tiny, dark bird's silhouette is just visible detaching from a distant mirror reflection, almost unnoticed.

chapters.title: Chapter Six: Ending
chapters.text:
The colossal Queen of Hearts shattered, not into reflections, but into splintering agony. The animated cards crumbled, their harsh edges slicing through the very air. Snow White's knees buckled, and the world spun violently, not with the whimsical tilt of a garden, but with the nauseating lurch of true vertigo. She fell, a clumsy, graceless heap, onto the cold, hard obsidian floor of the mirror room.

For a long moment, there was only the ringing silence in her ears, the acrid taste of fear, and the dull ache of bruised dignity. Her eyes, still wide with residual terror, darted about the room. The mirrors, blessedly, were static. The flickering candles held their ground. The monstrous Queen, the sneering Cat, the gibbering birds – all were gone. A profound, bone-deep weariness settled over her, a heavier weight than any dream.

Then, a chilling clarity. The apple. The relentless gnawing desire to be less. The voice of the Queen, echoing her stepmother's cruel pronouncements, had not been entirely an external torment. It had been her own mind, twisted by starvation, by the obsessive pursuit of an impossible ideal of slenderness. The hallucinations, the blurring lines, the terrifying certainty of her perceived flaws – they were not the effect of a magic apple, but the symptom of a deeper, self-inflicted poison. The apple had merely been the catalyst, the symbolic key to unlock the self-destruction she had already invited.

With a monumental effort, Snow White pushed herself up, her limbs trembling like brittle twigs. Her gaze, drawn by an irresistible, terrible compulsion, landed on the nearest mirror. It was not the rippling, distorting glass of her fevered nightmare, but a stark, unflinching surface. And in it, she saw herself. Not the rounded, soft girl she had once been, nor the grotesquely bloated caricature of her hallucinations, but something far more horrifying. Her face was gaunt, eyes sunken, skin a translucent pallor that made the dark circles beneath her eyes seem like bruises. Her once lush, raven hair now hung limp, lifeless. The delicate bones of her collarbone jutted sharply beneath her gown, and her wrists were shockingly fragile, almost skeletal.

This was not the slim, ethereal beauty she had so desperately chased. This was morbid. A grotesque parody of life, thin to the point of vanishing, yet undeniably present in its stark, cadaverous reality. The mirror, which had once shown her a distorted image of inadequacy, now reflected the grim, tangible consequence of her desperate flight from it. The truth, finally unvarnished, was a blade. She had carved away not just perceived flaws, but her very essence, leaving behind a stark, living effigy of self-denial. The room, silent and still, bore witness to the grim reality she had forged.
chapters.image_prompt:
Subject: Snow White, now with a slightly more gaunt face, jet-black hair, wearing a dark blue gothic fairytale dress, clutching the apple (now partially consumed).
Action: Looking with wide, disoriented eyes at a tiny, obsidian bird perched on the untouched ice cream sundae, its eye glinting with unsettling intelligence. In a nearby mirror reflection, her waistline appears subtly wider, her jawline softer than reality.
Setting: The mirror room, now with more pronounced warping in reflections. The ice cream sundae sits untouched on a mirrored pedestal.
Style: Gothic Illustration, Dark Fairytale Art, Surrealism, Chiaroscuro lighting, vivid hallucination against a real backdrop, highly detailed, cinematic, painterly.
Details: The bird is impossibly dark and still; the body dysmorphia in her reflection is subtle but disturbing, an insidious internal critique.
